title: Fish and Chips (Kadai Style)

ingredients.name: white fish fillets (tilapia, basa, pollock, or cod), boneless

subs.name: thick paneer slabs (400 g) for a vegetarian kadai fry — reduce frying time to 2–3 minutes
subs.notes: same batter and chip accompaniment work perfectly

subs.name: raw banana (vazhakkai) cut into chips — par-boil 5 minutes then double-fry as described
subs.notes: holds up well and stays crispier for longer

subs.name: half maida and half rice flour (no additional rice flour needed) — gives a thinner, crispier batter
subs.notes: adjust water to achieve the same consistency

ingredients.name: rice flour
visual_checks: adds extra crunch to the batter
ingredients.notes: adds extra crunch to the batter

subs.name: 2 tbsp thick curd whisked into the batter — gives a slightly denser but still good coating
subs.notes: the batter may need 1 tbsp extra water

ingredients.name: cold water
visual_checks: must be cold — warmth makes the batter dense
ingredients.notes: must be cold — warmth makes the batter dense

ingredients.name: lemon, cut into wedges for serving

steps.text: Peel the potatoes and cut into thick chips, about 2 cm wide and 7–8 cm long. Place in a large bowl of cold water and soak for 15 minutes to remove surface starch — this is what makes them crisp inside and fluffy within. Drain and pat completely dry with a cloth.

Make the batter: whisk together the maida, rice flour (if using), baking powder, black pepper and half a teaspoon of salt. Make a well in the centre, add the egg and pour in the cold water. Whisk until smooth — the batter should look like thick paint and coat the back of a spoon. Keep the batter cold; pop it in the fridge if the kitchen is warm.

Pour the oil into a deep kadai and heat over high flame. Test the temperature by dropping in a small chip — it should sizzle actively but not brown immediately (about 160–165°C). Add the chips in two batches and fry for 8–10 minutes, turning once or twice, until cooked through but still pale and soft. Drain on kitchen paper.

Increase the oil temperature until it shimmers and a drop of batter floats and sizzles immediately (about 180°C). Pat the fish fillets dry, season lightly with salt and pepper, then dip each fillet into the batter, letting the excess drip off for a second before lowering it carefully into the oil.

Fry the fish in batches (do not crowd the kadai) for 4–5 minutes per side until the batter is deep golden and the fish feels firm when pressed. Drain on kitchen paper and keep warm. Each fillet is done when the batter is crisp and doesn't deflect when tapped.

Return the blanched chips to the hot oil (which should be around 185°C now) and fry for 2–3 minutes until they are golden, crisp and irresistible. Work in batches to keep the oil hot. Drain, sprinkle with salt immediately.

Serve the fish and chips immediately — they do not improve with waiting. Arrange on a plate with lemon wedges on the side. Mint chutney or a squeeze of lime works beautifully as a dipping sauce in the Indian version.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → thick paneer slabs (400 g) for a vegetarian kadai fry — reduce frying time to 2–3 minutes (same batter and chip accompaniment work perfectly); maida → half maida and half rice flour (no additional rice flour needed) — gives a thinner, crispier batter (adjust water to achieve the same consistency); egg → 2 tbsp thick curd whisked into the batter — gives a slightly denser but still good coating (the batter may need 1 tbsp extra water); potato → raw banana (vazhakkai) cut into chips — par-boil 5 minutes then double-fry as described (holds up well and stays crispier for longer)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A deep kadai is the ideal Indian tool for this — wider than the traditional British chip pan but just as effective. Use basa or tilapia (widely available frozen in Indian cities), which are meaty, boneless fillets that hold up in hot oil. Add a pinch of red chilli powder or ajwain to the batter for a desi touch. Serve with mint-coriander chutney thinned with a little curd — it is a surprisingly natural match.
